topic: A person learns their college is closing down
raw_output:
[story 1]

The fluorescent lights in the lecture hall hummed with a sickly, persistent buzz, a sound that seemed amplified in the sudden silence. Sarah traced the condensation on her lukewarm coffee cup, the ceramic slick beneath her fingertips. Every time she glanced at the whiteboard, the neatly organized course schedule seemed to rearrange itself, the familiar names blurring into an unsettling jumble. She kept checking her phone, a nervous habit, though no notifications were coming through. A cold prickle started at the base of her neck, a sensation that made the fine hairs stand up. She pushed her chair back, the scrape of metal against the linoleum sounding deafeningly loud in the cavernous room. "Is this... is this true?" she whispered to the empty seat beside her, the question hanging brittle in the air.

[story 2]

Mark kept running his hand over the worn spine of his philosophy textbook, his knuckles whitening slightly. The campus bookstore, usually a haven of comforting, familiar smells—old paper and fresh ink—now felt strangely sterile, the aisles unnervingly empty. He kept turning to look at the campus map tacked to the bulletin board, his eyes snagging on the faded lines marking the administrative buildings. A tightness constricted his chest, making each breath shallow and quick. He stood up abruptly, needing to move, needing an anchor. When he finally looked up, the main entrance seemed dimmer than usual, and the usual flurry of student activity was absent, replaced by a heavy, stagnant quiet that pressed down on him.

[story 3]

I kept re-reading the official announcement, the words swimming before my eyes, refusing to settle into anything concrete. The air in my small dorm room felt thick, heavy, as if I were wading through water. I paced the small rug, my footsteps echoing hollowly against the bare walls. Each time I stopped, I’d press my palms against my eyes, trying to force the reality into a shape that made sense. My stomach churned, a tight, unpleasant knot forming just below my ribs. I walked to the window and looked out at the quad; it looked too quiet, the vibrant green of the lawns seeming muted, as if the world outside had suddenly muted its own volume.

Sarah traced the condensation on her lukewarm coffee cup, the ceramic slick beneath her fingertips. Every time she glanced at the whiteboard, the neatly organized course schedule seemed to rearrange itself, the familiar names blurring into an unsettling jumble. She pushed her chair back, the scrape of metal against the linoleum sounding deafeningly loud in the cavernous room.
